name: Nature Walk Observation Journal
description: Structured outdoor observation activity where learners spend 15 minutes in silent observation using all senses, then record what they noticed in a journal. Afterward, learners compare notes with partners to discover what they missed and what they noticed differently. Develops attention, systematic recording, and awareness that different observers notice different things.
learning_objectives: Use multiple senses intentionally during observation
Record observations with detail and accuracy
Distinguish between what was observed and what was inferred
Recognize that different observers notice different things
variations: Seasonal comparison: repeat the same walk in different seasons and compare journals
Sensory focus: one walk emphasizing sound, another emphasizing touch or smell
Urban variation: observe a city block instead of a natural area

name: Mystery Box Sensory Investigation
description: Sealed boxes containing unknown objects. Learners must determine what is inside using only indirect evidence: shaking, weighing, tilting, listening, and smelling. They record observations, form hypotheses about contents, and discuss the difference between evidence and guessing. Models the scientific challenge of investigating things that cannot be directly seen.
materials: Various small objects to place inside
learning_objectives: Gather evidence using indirect methods
Distinguish between observation and inference
Form hypotheses based on available evidence
Understand that some questions require indirect investigation
variations: Difficulty levels: single objects vs. multiple objects in one box
Competitive version: teams race to identify contents with fewest observations
Create-your-own: learners prepare mystery boxes for classmates

name: Paper Airplane Hypothesis Test
description: Learners design paper airplanes with systematic variations (wing shape, paper weight, fold count, nose style) and test how each variable affects flight distance. They formulate specific hypotheses before each test, identify variables to control, conduct multiple trials, and analyze results. Demonstrates the full hypothesis-experiment-analysis cycle with tangible, engaging materials.
materials: Paper of different weights and sizes
Tape or paper clips for weight adjustment
learning_objectives: Formulate specific, testable hypotheses about flight performance
Identify independent, dependent, and controlled variables
Conduct multiple trials for reliability
Analyze results to evaluate hypotheses
variations: Target accuracy challenge: test distance vs. accuracy as dependent variables
Advanced: add wing flaps or tail modifications as additional variables
Engineering extension: optimize for maximum distance using experimental results

name: Plant Growth Experiment Design
description: Learners design a controlled experiment comparing plant growth under different conditions (light levels, water amounts, soil types, or fertilizer concentrations). They must identify all variables, explain what they will control and why, predict outcomes, and create a data collection plan. The design phase is the primary learning activity; actual growing extends over weeks with periodic data collection.
learning_objectives: Design a controlled experiment with one independent variable
Identify and plan controls for confounding variables
Create a detailed data collection plan before beginning
Make specific predictions about expected outcomes
variations: Quick version: use fast-germinating seeds like radish for 2-week experiments
Advanced: compare two variables across multiple experimental groups
Hydroponic variation: grow in water with different nutrient solutions

name: Weather Data Collection Week
description: Over five consecutive days, learners collect weather data at the same time each day: temperature, cloud cover (estimated percentage), precipitation (yes/no and amount), wind direction, and wind speed (estimated scale). They organize data in tables, create graphs showing trends, and compare their measurements with official weather service data to assess accuracy. Teaches systematic data collection, organization, and graphing.
materials: Access to official weather data for comparison
learning_objectives: Collect data systematically over multiple days
Organize data in structured tables
Create appropriate graphs to visualize trends
Compare personal measurements with official data to assess accuracy
variations: Extended: collect data for a full month and analyze longer-term patterns
Cross-location: compare data with a partner class in a different region
Prediction component: predict next day's weather based on observed patterns

name: Pendulum Period Measurement
description: Learners build a simple pendulum (string and weight) and measure its period under systematically varied conditions: string length, mass of weight, and release angle. They discover which variable actually affects the period (length) and which do not (mass, small-amplitude changes). Emphasizes multiple trials, averaging, precision in measurement, and the power of controlled experimentation to reveal unexpected results.
learning_objectives: Measure time intervals with precision using multiple trials
Calculate averages and assess variability in measurements
Distinguish which variables affect an outcome and which do not
Draw conclusions that may contradict initial expectations
variations: Graphing extension: plot period vs. length and discover the square-root relationship
Prediction challenge: predict the period for an untested length based on the pattern
Historical connection: discuss Galileo's pendulum observations and their significance

name: Science Fair Poster Creation
description: Learners create a poster summarizing a completed investigation, focusing on visual hierarchy, clear data presentation, and concise writing. The poster must include: title, question, hypothesis, method summary, data display (graph or table), conclusion, and reflection on limitations. After creation, a structured peer review round provides constructive feedback. Develops scientific communication and visual literacy.
learning_objectives: Organize scientific information in a clear visual layout
Present data effectively using appropriate graphs and tables
Write concise summaries of methods and conclusions
Give and receive constructive peer feedback
variations: Digital version: create an electronic poster or infographic
Gallery walk: display all posters and have learners evaluate three peers using a rubric
Lightning talk: present the poster in a 3-minute oral presentation

name: Peer Review Practice
description: Learners receive anonymous investigation reports (from partners or provided examples) and provide structured feedback using a review rubric. They evaluate: clarity of question and hypothesis, quality of experimental design, appropriateness of data analysis, strength of conclusions, and acknowledgment of limitations. The original author then revises based on feedback. Teaches that peer review improves science and that revision is a strength.
materials: Peer review rubric with scoring criteria
learning_objectives: Evaluate scientific work using specific quality criteria
Provide constructive, evidence-based feedback
Distinguish between helpful critique and personal opinion
Revise own work in response to peer feedback
variations: Double-blind review: both author and reviewer are anonymous
Review published studies: apply the rubric to a simplified real research paper
Meta-review: evaluate the quality of peer reviews themselves

name: Science vs. Not-Science Sorting
description: Learners receive cards with various claims (homeopathy, evolution, flat earth, germ theory, astrology, vaccine safety, crystal healing, plate tectonics, climate change) and sort them into scientific vs. non-scientific categories. They must justify each placement using specific demarcation criteria: testability, falsifiability, peer review, reproducibility, and self-correction. Develops critical evaluation skills and understanding of what makes science distinct.
materials: Sorting mat with 'Scientific' and 'Not Scientific' columns
learning_objectives: Apply demarcation criteria to evaluate real-world claims
Articulate what makes a claim scientific vs. non-scientific
Discuss borderline cases with nuance and evidence
Understand that science is defined by method, not by topic
variations: Current events version: sort claims from recent news articles
Historical version: evaluate claims that were once considered scientific but are no longer
Create-your-own: learners write ambiguous claims for classmates to evaluate

name: Claim-Evidence-Reasoning Exercises
description: Given a scientific question and a data set, learners construct written arguments using the CER framework: make a Claim that answers the question, cite specific Evidence from the data, and provide Reasoning that explains why the evidence supports the claim. Examples range from simple ('Does ice melt faster in water or air?') to complex ('Is this pattern in the data evidence of a correlation?'). Scaffolds scientific argumentation skills.
materials: Scientific questions at varying difficulty levels
learning_objectives: Construct claims that directly answer scientific questions
Cite specific evidence from data to support claims
Write reasoning that logically connects evidence to claims
Evaluate the strength of CER arguments
variations: Jigsaw CER: different groups analyze different data sets about the same question
Counter-argument: write a CER, then write a counterargument and rebuttal
Visual CER: present the argument as a diagram with linked claim-evidence-reasoning boxes
